question: A galvanometer G of 2Ω resistance is connected in the given circuit. The ratio of charge stored in C1 and C2 is:
solution:
In steady state:

 Req=12 Ω

Current through the branches with resistance, I=612=0.5 A

Now, potential drop across C1=3 V

and potential drop C2=4 V

Charge on the first capacitor, q1=C1 V1=12 μC

Charge on the second capacitor, q2=C2 V2=24 μC

Therefore, q1q2=12

subject: Physics
q: Three capacitors of capacitances $25 \mu \mathrm{F}, 30 \mu \mathrm{F}$ and $45 \mu \mathrm{F}$ are connected in parallel to a supply of $100 \mathrm{~V}$. Energy stored in the above combination is E. When these capacitors are connected in series to the same supply, the stored energy is $\frac{9}{x} \mathrm{E}$. The value of $x$ is _____.
question: Three capacitors of capacitances $25 \mu \mathrm{F}, 30 \mu \mathrm{F}$ and $45 \mu \mathrm{F}$ are connected in parallel to a supply of $100 \mathrm{~V}$. Energy stored in the above combination is E. When these capacitors are connected in series to the same supply, the stored energy is $\frac{9}{x} \mathrm{E}$. The value of $x$ is _____.
solution:
In parallel combination : Potential difference is same across all
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Energy }=\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{C}_1+\mathrm{C}_2+\mathrm{C}_3\right) \mathrm{V}^2 \\
& =\frac{1}{2}(25+30+45) \times(100)^2 \times 10^{-6}=0.5=\mathrm{E}
\end{aligned}$
In series combination: Charge is same on all.
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{\text {equ }}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_2}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_3}=\frac{1}{25}+\frac{1}{30}+\frac{1}{45} \\
& \frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{\text {equ }}}=\frac{(18+15+10)}{450}=\frac{43}{450} \Rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{\text {equ }}=\frac{450}{43} \\
& \text { Energy }=\frac{\mathrm{Q}^2}{2 \mathrm{C}_1}+\frac{\mathrm{Q}^2}{2 \mathrm{C}_2}+\frac{\mathrm{Q}^2}{2 \mathrm{C}_3} \\
& =\frac{\mathrm{Q}^2}{2}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_2}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_3}\right] \\
& \frac{\left(\mathrm{V} \times \mathrm{C}_{\text {equ }}\right)^2}{2} \times \frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{\text {equ }}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^2 \mathrm{C}_{\text {equ }}}{2} \\
& \frac{(100)^2}{2} \times \frac{450}{43} \times 10^{-6} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{4.5}{86}=\frac{9}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{E}=\frac{9}{\mathrm{x}} \times 0.5 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=86
\end{aligned}$

q: Consider the combination of two capacitors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>></mo><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, when connected in parallel, the equivalent capacitance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> times the equivalent capacitance of the same connected in series. Calculate the ratio of capacitors, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfrac></math>.
question: Consider the combination of two capacitors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>></mo><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, when connected in parallel, the equivalent capacitance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> times the equivalent capacitance of the same connected in series. Calculate the ratio of capacitors, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfrac></math>.
solution:
When connected in parallel

Ceq=C1+C2

When in series

Ceq'=C1C2C1+C2

C1+C2=10C1C2C1+C2

C1+C22=10C1C2

C12+C22-8C1C2=0

dividing by C12

C2C12-8C2C1+1=0

Let C2C1=x

x2-8x+1=0

x=8±64-42=4+15

This question was Bonus in JEE Main 2021 exam because of incorrect data in original question.

=c×c22c+c2V0-02=C6V02

subject: Physics
q: A sphere of capacitance $100$ pF is charged to a potential of $100$ V. Another identical uncharged metal sphere is brought in contact with the charged sphere, then the change in the total energy stored on these spheres, when they touch is $\alpha \times 10^{-7}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is __________. (combined capacitance of spheres is $200$ pF)
question: A sphere of capacitance $100$ pF is charged to a potential of $100$ V. Another identical uncharged metal sphere is brought in contact with the charged sphere, then the change in the total energy stored on these spheres, when they touch is $\alpha \times 10^{-7}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is __________. (combined capacitance of spheres is $200$ pF)
solution:
Initial energy of the charged sphere is given by:

$U_i = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$

$U_i = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 100 \times 10^{-12} \times (100)^2 = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ J

When the identical uncharged sphere is brought in contact, the charge is shared equally and the common potential becomes:

$V' = \dfrac{V}{2} = 50$ V

Final total energy of the two spheres is:

$U_f = 2 \times \left( \dfrac{1}{2} C V'^2 \right) = C V'^2$

$U_f = 100 \times 10^{-12} \times (50)^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}$ J

Change in energy is the loss of energy during charge sharing:

$\Delta U = U_i - U_f = 5 \times 10^{-7} - 2.5 \times 10^{-7} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}$ J

$\Delta U = \dfrac{5}{2} \times 10^{-7}$ J

Comparing with $\alpha \times 10^{-7}$ J, we get $\alpha = \dfrac{5}{2}$.

Answer: $\dfrac{5}{2}$

subject: Physics
q: Capacitance of an isolated conducting sphere of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> times when it is enclosed by a concentric conducting sphere of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> connected to earth. The ratio of their radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math> is:
question: Capacitance of an isolated conducting sphere of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> times when it is enclosed by a concentric conducting sphere of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> connected to earth. The ratio of their radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math> is:
solution:
Capacitance of isolated conducting sphere C=4πε0R1

By enclosing inside another sphere of radius R2, new capacitance C'=4πε0R1R2R2-R1

Given: 4πε0R1R2R2-R1=n×4πε0R1

⇒R2R2-R1=n⇒R2R1R2R1-1=n

⇒R2R1=nR2R1-n⇒R2R1=nn-1

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Two metallic spheres are charged to the same potential. One of them is hollow and another is solid, and both have the same radii. Solid sphere will have lower charge than the hollow one.

Reason R : Capacitance of metallic spheres depend on the radii of spheres.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements : One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Two metallic spheres are charged to the same potential. One of them is hollow and another is solid, and both have the same radii. Solid sphere will have lower charge than the hollow one.

Reason R : Capacitance of metallic spheres depend on the radii of spheres.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: A is false but R is true
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
solution:
Potential of a conducting sphere is given by V=KQR 

As in case of metallic sphere either solid or hollow, the charge will reside on the surface of the sphere. Since both spheres have same surface area, so they can hold equal charge.

∴Q1=Q2

The capacitance of a metallic sphere depends only on its radius. It does not matter whether the sphere is hollow or solid.

Hence, A is false but R is true.

At equilibrium, the charge on the capacitor C2 is _____ μC.
solution:
The initial charge on the 5 μF capacitor will be q=CV=150 μC. If the charge on the 10 μF capacitor is q after the key is closed then,

150-q5=q10⇒q=100 μC

The space between one of the capacitors is filled with dielectric material of dielectric constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math> such that the equivalence capacitance of the system became <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math> will be :
solution:
When a dielectric is inserted between the sheets of a capacitor, its capacitance becomes KC.

Now the equivalent capacitance in series combination will be,

Ceq=C×KCC1+K⇒24=40K1+K⇒K=1.5

=12Cvo2-212CV022
=CVo24
=60×10-122024J

subject: Physics
q:
A parallel plate capacitor with area $200 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ and separation between the plates $1.5 \mathrm{~cm}$, is connected across a battery of emf V. If the force of attraction between the plates is $25 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~N}$, the value of $V$ is approximately:
$$
\left.\left(\varepsilon_0=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \frac{\mathrm{C}^2}{\text { N.m }}\right)^2\right)
$$
question:
A parallel plate capacitor with area $200 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ and separation between the plates $1.5 \mathrm{~cm}$, is connected across a battery of emf V. If the force of attraction between the plates is $25 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~N}$, the value of $V$ is approximately:
$$
\left.\left(\varepsilon_0=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \frac{\mathrm{C}^2}{\text { N.m }}\right)^2\right)
$$
solution:
Given area of Parallel plate capacitor, $A=$ $200 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ Separation between the plates, $d=1.5 \mathrm{~cm}$ Force of attraction between the plates, $F=$ $25 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~N}$ $F=Q E$ $F=\frac{Q^2}{2 A \epsilon_0} \quad$ (E due to parallel plate $\left.=\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_0}=\frac{Q}{A 2 \epsilon_0}\right)$ But $Q=C V=\frac{\in_0 A(V)}{d}$
$$ \begin{gathered} \left.\therefore F=\frac{\left(\epsilon_0 A V^2\right)}{\mathrm{d}^2 \times 2 \mathrm{~A} \epsilon_0}\right) \\ =\frac{\left(\epsilon_0 A\right)^2 \times V^2}{d^2 \times 2 \times\left(A \epsilon_0\right)}=\frac{\left(\epsilon_0 A\right) \times V^2}{d^2 \times 2} \\ \text { or, } 25 \times 10^{-6}=\frac{\left(8.85 \times 10^{-12}\right) \times\left(200 \times 10^{-4}\right) \times V^2}{2.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 2} \\ \Rightarrow V=\frac{25 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 2}{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 200 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 250 \mathrm{~V} \end{gathered} $$

subject: Physics
q: In the circuit shown, the energy stored in the capacitor is n μJ. The value of n is _____.
question: In the circuit shown, the energy stored in the capacitor is n μJ. The value of n is _____.
solution:
The capacitor works as an open circuit in the given configuration.

The potential difference between the two plates of the capacitor will be the difference across the resistors having resistances 3 Ω and 4 Ω.

Hence, the potential difference ∆V across the capacitor can be calculated as follows:

ΔV=46×12-312×12 V=5 V

The formula to calculate the energy stored in the capacitor is given by

U=12C(∆V)2   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the required energy stored.

U=12×6 μF×5 V2=75 μJ

q:
In the given circuit.C1=2μF,C2=0.2μF,C3=2μF,C4=4μF,C5=
2μF,C6=2μF. The charge stored on capacitor C4 is  ______   μC.

2μF,C6=2μF. The charge stored on capacitor C4 is  ______   μC.
solution:
From the diagram of the circuit, C3, C4, C5 are in series, so the total capacitance is 

1C'=12+14+12⇒C'=45 μF

C' & C2 are parallel, the total capacitance is 

C''=45+0.2 μF=1 μF

C1, C'', C6 are in series. So the equivalent capacitance is,

1Ceq=12+1+12⇒Ceq=0.5 μF

Using Q=CeqV.

The charge through the battery, Q =10×12=5 μC

 From the diagram above,

Q'=5μC×0.80.8+0.2=4 μC

Ui=12K1C0V2 and Uf=12K2C0V2

⇒ΔU=Uf-Ui=12K2-K1C0V2

Percentage change in the energy will be,

ΔUUi×100=12×5×C0212×10×C02×100=50%

subject: Physics
q: The dimensional formula of $\dfrac{1}{2}\epsilon_0 E^2$ ($\epsilon_0$ = permittivity of vacuum and $E$ = electric field) is $M^a L^b T^c$. The value of $2a - b + c = $ _______.
question: The dimensional formula of $\dfrac{1}{2}\epsilon_0 E^2$ ($\epsilon_0$ = permittivity of vacuum and $E$ = electric field) is $M^a L^b T^c$. The value of $2a - b + c = $ _______.
solution:
The quantity $\dfrac{1}{2}\epsilon_0 E^2$ represents the energy density of an electric field.

Energy density is defined as energy per unit volume.

Dimensional formula of energy is $[M L^2 T^{-2}]$.

Dimensional formula of volume is $[L^3]$.

Dimensional formula of energy density $= \dfrac{[M L^2 T^{-2}]}{[L^3]} = [M L^{-1} T^{-2}]$.

Comparing this with $M^a L^b T^c$, we get:
$a = 1$
$b = -1$
$c = -2$

The value of $2a - b + c = 2(1) - (-1) + (-2) = 2 + 1 - 2 = 1$.

Answer: $1$

subject: Physics
q: Let $C$ be the capacitance of a capacitor discharging through a resistor R. Suppose $t_1$ is the time taken for the energy stored in the capacitor to reduce to half its initial value and $t_2$ is the time taken for the charge to reduce to one-fourth its initial value. Then the ratio $t_1 / t_2$ will be
question: Let $C$ be the capacitance of a capacitor discharging through a resistor R. Suppose $t_1$ is the time taken for the energy stored in the capacitor to reduce to half its initial value and $t_2$ is the time taken for the charge to reduce to one-fourth its initial value. Then the ratio $t_1 / t_2$ will be
solution:
$\mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{q}^2}{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{C}}\left(\mathrm{q}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t} / \mathrm{T}}\right)^2=\frac{\mathrm{q}_0^2}{2 \mathrm{C}} \mathrm{e}^{-2 \mathrm{t} / \mathrm{T}} \quad($ where $\tau=\mathrm{CR})$
$\mathrm{U}=\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{e}^{-2 \mathrm{t} / \mathrm{\tau}}$
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{e}^{-2 \mathrm{t}_1 / \mathrm{\tau}}$
$\frac{1}{2}=\mathrm{e}^{-2 \mathrm{t}_1 / \tau} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_1=\frac{\mathrm{T}}{2} \ln 2$
Now $\quad \mathrm{q}=\mathrm{q}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t} / \mathrm{T}}$
$\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{q}_0=\mathrm{q}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t} / 2 \mathrm{~T}}$
$\mathrm{t}_2=\operatorname{T} \ln 4=2 \operatorname{T} \ln 2$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{t}_1}{\mathrm{t}_2}=\frac{1}{4}$

q: A parallel plate capacitor with width <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and separation between the plates of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> is connected to a battery of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. A dielectric slab of dielectric constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> having length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, width <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> is inserted between the plates of parallel plate capacitor. The electrostatic energy of this system will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ϵ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. (Where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ϵ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is the permittivity of free space)
question: A parallel plate capacitor with width <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and separation between the plates of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> is connected to a battery of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. A dielectric slab of dielectric constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> having length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, width <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> is inserted between the plates of parallel plate capacitor. The electrostatic energy of this system will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ϵ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. (Where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ϵ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is the permittivity of free space)
solution:
The figure below shows the parallel plate capacitor with dielectric slab placed in between the plates.

Now, the effective capacitance of the combination is 

Ceff=ε07×4410+5ε01×4410×10-2

=70ε0+50ε0×10-2=120ε0×10-2

⇒Ceff =1.2ε0

Electrostatics energy of this system is =12CeffV2

=121.2ε02020=240ε0

subject: Physics
q: A parallel plate capacitor is having separation between plates $0.885$ mm. It has a capacitance of $1\,\mu$F when the space between the plates is filled with an insulating material of resistivity $1\times 10^{13}\,\Omega$m and resistance $17.7\times 10^{14}\,\Omega$. Relative permittivity of the insulating material is $\alpha\times 10^7$. The value of $\alpha$ is ________. (Take permittivity of free space $=8.85\times 10^{-12}$ F/m)
question: A parallel plate capacitor is having separation between plates $0.885$ mm. It has a capacitance of $1\,\mu$F when the space between the plates is filled with an insulating material of resistivity $1\times 10^{13}\,\Omega$m and resistance $17.7\times 10^{14}\,\Omega$. Relative permittivity of the insulating material is $\alpha\times 10^7$. The value of $\alpha$ is ________. (Take permittivity of free space $=8.85\times 10^{-12}$ F/m)
solution:
The resistance of the insulating material is given by $R = \dfrac{\rho d}{A}$.

The capacitance of the parallel plate capacitor is given by $C = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A}{d}$.

Multiplying the expressions for $R$ and $C$, we get:

$RC = \rho \epsilon_0 \epsilon_r$

Rearranging for relative permittivity $\epsilon_r$:

$\epsilon_r = \dfrac{RC}{\rho \epsilon_0}$

Substituting the given values:

$\epsilon_r = \dfrac{17.7 \times 10^{14} \times 10^{-6}}{10^{13} \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12}}$

$\epsilon_r = \dfrac{17.7 \times 10^8}{8.85 \times 10^1}$

$\epsilon_r = 2 \times 10^7$

Comparing this with the given expression $\epsilon_r = \alpha \times 10^7$, we get:

$\alpha = 2$

Answer: $2$

subject: Physics
q: As shown in the figure, two parallel plate capacitors having equal plate area of 200 cm2 are joined in such a way that a≠b. The equivalent capacitance of the combination is xε0F. The value of x is _____.
question: As shown in the figure, two parallel plate capacitors having equal plate area of 200 cm2 are joined in such a way that a≠b. The equivalent capacitance of the combination is xε0F. The value of x is _____.
solution:
The given data is 

A=200×10-4 m2d=5 ×10-3 m

The value of a+b=4×10-3 m

The formula for a parallel plate capacitor is 

C=ε0AD

Let the capacitor with gap a be C1 and capacitor with gap b be C2

C1=ε0Aa, C2=ε0Ab

The equivalent capacitance is 

1Ceq=aε0A+bε0A⇒Ceq=ε0Aa+b

It is given that Ceq=xε0 F

So, 

ε0Aa+b=xε0⇒x=Aa+b⇒x=200×10-44×10-3=5

q: If there are n capacitors in parallel connected to V volt source, then the energy stored is equal to
question: If there are n capacitors in parallel connected to V volt source, then the energy stored is equal to
solution: $E=\sum \frac{1}{2} C V^2=\frac{1}{2} n C V^2$

question: A capacitor is made of two square plates each of side ‘ a ’ making a very small angle α between them, as shown in figure. The capacitance will be close to:

subject: Physics
q: A parallel plate capacitor has charge $5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C}$. A dielectric slab is inserted between the plates and almost fills the space between the plates. If the induced charge on one face of the slab is $4 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C}$ then the dielectric constant of the slab is ________.
question: A parallel plate capacitor has charge $5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C}$. A dielectric slab is inserted between the plates and almost fills the space between the plates. If the induced charge on one face of the slab is $4 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C}$ then the dielectric constant of the slab is ________.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{in}}=\mathrm{Q}\left(1-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}}\right) \\ & 4 \times 10^{-6}=5 \times 10^{-6}\left(1-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}}\right) \\ & 1-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}}=\frac{4}{5} \\ & \mathrm{~K}=5\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A parallel plate capacitor is made of two square plates of side a, separated by a distance d d≪a. The lower triangular portion filled with a dielectric of dielectric constant K, as shown in the figure. Capacitance of this capacitor is:
question: A parallel plate capacitor is made of two square plates of side a, separated by a distance d d≪a. The lower triangular portion filled with a dielectric of dielectric constant K, as shown in the figure. Capacitance of this capacitor is:
solution:
Consider a small element at a distance x. It is a two capacitors in series.

dc=dc1×dc2dc1+dc2

dc=ε0.adxxtanθk+d-xtanθ1

∴dc=ε0K.adx-xtan⁡θK-1+d.k

∴C=∫0aε0K.adx-xtan⁡θK-1+d.k

=ε0k.atan⁡θ(K-1)ln -atan⁡θK-1 +d.kd.K

=ε0Ka2dK-1ln K

subject: Physics
q: A parallel plate capacitor has plate area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and plates separation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The space between the plates is filled with a dielectric medium of a thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and dielectric constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math>. The capacitance of the system is:
question: A parallel plate capacitor has plate area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and plates separation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The space between the plates is filled with a dielectric medium of a thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and dielectric constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math>. The capacitance of the system is:
solution:
With insertion of dielectric, the system can be considered as combination of two capacitors in series.

Thus, equivalent capacitance of the system is 

1Ceq=1C1+1C2

=1Kε0 At+1ε0 A d-t

=tKε0 A+d-tε0 A

=1×10-35ε0×40×10-4+1×10-3ε0×40×10-4

⇒1Ceq=120ε0+14ε0

⇒Ceq=20×4ε024=10ε03 F.

subject: Physics
q: Three parallel plate capacitors $\mathrm{C}_1, \mathrm{C}_2$ and $\mathrm{C}_3$ each of capacitance $5 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ are connected as shown in figure. The effective capacitance between points A and B, when the space between the parallel plates of $C_1$ capacitor is filled with a dielectric medium having dielectric constant of 4, is :
question: Three parallel plate capacitors $\mathrm{C}_1, \mathrm{C}_2$ and $\mathrm{C}_3$ each of capacitance $5 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ are connected as shown in figure. The effective capacitance between points A and B, when the space between the parallel plates of $C_1$ capacitor is filled with a dielectric medium having dielectric constant of 4, is :
solution:
After dielectric
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{C}_1=4 \mathrm{C} \\ & \mathrm{C}_1=4 \times 5=20 \mu \mathrm{~F} \\ & \mathrm{C}_2=\mathrm{C}_3=5 \mu \mathrm{~F}
\end{aligned}$
$\mathrm{C}_1 \& \mathrm{C}_2$ are in series which is parallel to $\mathrm{C}_3$ So
$\begin{aligned}
& C_{c q}=\frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1+C_2}+C_3 \Rightarrow \frac{20 \times 5}{20+5}+5 \\ & =4+5=9 \mu \mathrm{~F}
\end{aligned}$
Correct Option (3)

subject: Physics
q: A resistor 'R' and $2 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor in series is connected through a switch to $200 \mathrm{~V}$ direct supply. Across the capacitor is a neon bulb that lights up at $120 \mathrm{~V}$. Calculate the value of $R$ to make the bulb light up $5 \mathrm{~s}$ after the switch has been closed. $\left(\log _{10} 2.5=0.4\right)$
question: A resistor 'R' and $2 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor in series is connected through a switch to $200 \mathrm{~V}$ direct supply. Across the capacitor is a neon bulb that lights up at $120 \mathrm{~V}$. Calculate the value of $R$ to make the bulb light up $5 \mathrm{~s}$ after the switch has been closed. $\left(\log _{10} 2.5=0.4\right)$
solution:
$V_c=E\left(1-e^{-t / R c}\right)$
$1-e^{-t / R c}=\frac{120}{200}=\frac{3}{5}$
$\Rightarrow R=\frac{5}{1.84 \times 10^{-6}}=2.7 \times 10^6 \Omega$

subject: Physics
q: A condenser of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> capacitance is charged steadily from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Which of the following graph represents correctly the variation of potential difference<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>V</mi></mfenced></math> across its plates with respect to the charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>Q</mi></mfenced></math> on the condenser?
question: A condenser of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> capacitance is charged steadily from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Which of the following graph represents correctly the variation of potential difference<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>V</mi></mfenced></math> across its plates with respect to the charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>Q</mi></mfenced></math> on the condenser?
solution:
The charge on a condenser is given by Q=CV.

⇒V=1CQ

Since the capacitance depends on the construction of the condenser, it is constant. Therefore, the graph will be straight line with slope =1C.
Slope =1C=12×10-6=5×105

subject: Physics
q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> wire is bend to form a square loop. A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery with internal resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is connected across one of its sides. If a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> capacitor is connected across one of its diagonals, the energy stored by the capacitor will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>μJ</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>______.
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> wire is bend to form a square loop. A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery with internal resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is connected across one of its sides. If a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> capacitor is connected across one of its diagonals, the energy stored by the capacitor will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>μJ</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>______.
solution:
Under the balanced state, there is no flow of charge through the capacitor. So, the path connecting the capacitor behaves as an open path.

The equivalent resistance of the entire circuit, under equilibrium condition, can be calculated as follows:

Req=1+114+4+4+14 Ω=1+3 Ω=4 Ω

Hence, the current through the entire circuit is given by

I=VReq=94 A

So, the current I1 can be written as

I1=94×416 A=916 A

The potential difference between the points A and B is,

VA-VB=I1×8=916×8=92 V

Hence, the energy stored in the capacitor is given by

U=12CVA-VB2=12×4×814 μJ=812 μJ

∴x=81
